turns.user_message: I smoke bidis. About 25 a day for the last 45 years. I've reduced a little now because the doctor here keeps telling me to.
step2_rag_chunks: Bidi smoke is more concentrated than cigarette smoke and delivers higher levels of nicotine despite being thinner. This means bidi dependence can be as strong as or stronger than cigarette dependence, and withdrawal symptoms can be equally intense. Standard cessation support â€” behavioral strategies and nicotine replacement â€” is just as applicable and effective for bidi smokers.
Within weeks of quitting smoking, breathing and circulation often improve.
The 4D technique helps manage cravings: Delay smoking for a few minutes, take Deep breaths slowly, Drink water, and Do something else to distract yourself.

step4_session_phase.phase_instruction: You are in the EXPLORATION phase. You know the basics. Now gently explore what situations or emotions make them smoke more — triggers like stress, meals, social settings, boredom. Do NOT suggest any strategies yet. Just understand their situation.

step2_rag_chunks: There are two kinds of triggers â€” situational and emotional. Situational triggers are external: after meals, with tea, during a work break. Emotional triggers are internal: stress, boredom, loneliness, anxiety. Most people have both. Knowing which type is stronger for you helps us choose the right approach.
Taking a short walk or doing light physical activity can help reduce stress and distract from cravings.
Keeping a diary of triggers and cravings can help identify patterns and develop better coping strategies.

step2_rag_chunks: There are two kinds of triggers â€” situational and emotional. Situational triggers are external: after meals, with tea, during a work break. Emotional triggers are internal: stress, boredom, loneliness, anxiety. Most people have both. Knowing which type is stronger for you helps us choose the right approach.
Taking a short walk or doing light physical activity can help reduce stress and distract from cravings.

It is completely normal to want to quit and also feel unsure about it at the same time. Most people feel this way â€” one part of them wants to stop, another part is not ready. That conflict is not weakness, it is just how change works. It helps to talk about both sides openly rather than pushing the doubts aside.

step2_rag_chunks: If someone offers you a cigarette, practice politely saying no and reminding yourself why you decided to quit.

After meals, immediately introduce a competing behavior before the urge has a chance to form â€” get up from the table, brush your teeth, chew sugar-free gum, or take a short walk. The goal is to break the automatic link between finishing a meal and reaching for a cigarette by making a different behavior equally automatic over time.
Exercise can help improve mood and reduce nicotine cravings.
